narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 82.9% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your ranking in Year 3 grades at school, where you placed in the bottom decile—far below the top-half average seen among students who went on to pass the bar. This low Year 3 performance was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong academic momentum. Next in influence was your Year 1 grade decile, also in the lower ranks compared to the much higher standings typical of bar passers, further reinforcing the model's concerns about early academic struggles. Your undergraduate GPA offered some counterbalance, being somewhat closer to the averages of successful students and mildly pulling the prediction toward a passing outcome, though not enough to offset the other red flags. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of bar passers and your plan to attend full-time matches the vast majority of them, these didn't carry as much weight in your specific case. Your LSAT score of 27 also stood out as notably below the 37 average for those who passed, contributing to the overall risk profile. Compared to typical accepted students, who often enter with stronger decile rankings and higher test scores, your profile appeared riskier in the areas the model weighs most heavily for long-term success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call focused purely on bar exam predictors like grades and scores. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying once you've addressed these key areas, as many students improve their profiles and succeed on later attempts.
